title: Ten Movies to See Right Now. Plus, James Gray Returns!
format: Sean and Amanda work through the fall slate, from Armageddon Time and Aftersun to a Netflix true-crime dud and a stop-motion disappointment, before James Gray returns for his fourth trip on the show to talk about turning his own childhood into a movie.

segments.heading: The Speed Round: What to See Right Now
segments.note: a rapid tour of the fall slate

films.title: Armageddon Time
films.note: James Gray's most autobiographical film, built from his own Queens childhood in 1980. Jeremy Strong, Anne Hathaway, and Anthony Hopkins play members of his family, with newcomer Banks Repeta as his stand-in. Both hosts call it one of the best of the year.

films.title: Aftersun
films.note: Charlotte Wells's Cannes-acclaimed debut, with Paul Mescal and Frankie Corio as a father and daughter on a memory-haunted vacation. Sean loved it; Amanda respected it more than she connected with it.

films.title: The Good Nurse
films.note: Jessica Chastain and Eddie Redmayne in a true-crime hospital thriller directed by Tobias Lindholm. Amanda calls it a glossy Lifetime movie; Sean can't figure out why anyone signed on.

films.title: Wendell & Wild
films.note: Henry Selick's first stop-motion feature since Coraline, co-written by and starring Jordan Peele and Keegan-Michael Key. Sean finds it overstuffed and a disappointment after a 13-year wait.

films.title: Coraline
films.note: The bar Sean measures Wendell & Wild against: 'one of the most beautiful movies of the last 25 years,' he says, and a much simpler story.

films.title: Stars at Noon
films.note: Claire Denis adapts Denis Johnson's supposedly unadaptable novel, with Margaret Qualley and Joe Alwyn as two lost Americans in a Central American revolution. Sean's pick of the week; Amanda's more skeptical.

films.title: Rosaline
films.note: Kaitlyn Dever plays Romeo's spurned ex in this Shakespeare-adjacent teen comedy. Amanda likes the premise more than the execution.

films.title: The Stranger
films.note: Thomas M. Wright's slow-burn thriller with Sean Harris and Joel Edgerton, based on a real Mr. Big-style undercover sting. Sean calls it 'all the quiet parts of Heat.'

films.title: Catherine Called Birdy
films.note: Lena Dunham adapts the YA novel, with Bella Ramsey as a 14th-century teenager being married off by her family. Amanda calls it a total delight.

films.title: Holy Spider
films.note: Ali Abbasi's dramatization of the killer who murdered sex workers in Iran. Sean admires the craft but thinks it splits the difference between two different movies.

films.title: Louis Armstrong's Black & Blues
films.note: Sasha Jenkins's Apple-produced documentary, mentioned in passing as part of the fall pileup.

films.title: Descendant
films.note: Margaret Brown's Netflix documentary about the Clotilda, the last known ship to bring enslaved people to the U.S. Sean thinks it's a real contender for best documentary.

segments.heading: On the Radar
segments.note: what they're most excited to see next

films.title: She Said
films.note: Amanda's already seen it and is keeping her thoughts to herself for now.

films.title: The Fabelmans
films.note: Sean's excited for Spielberg's own coming-of-age film.

films.title: Glass Onion: A Knives Out Mystery
Black Panther: Wakanda Forever
films.note: Sean cites strong reviews out of the premiere.

films.title: The Banshees of Inisherin
films.note: Sean calls it one of the best movies released so far this year. Martin McDonagh joins the show the following week to talk about it.

films.title: Armageddon Time
films.note: Gray on shooting his own childhood: real family photos as reference, brisket cooking on set for the smell, and casting Hopkins, Hathaway, and Strong so the boy's parents would feel mythic to the audience the way his own did to him at 11.

films.title: Citizen Kane
films.note: Gray's touchstone for productive ambiguity: Welles never wanted to explain Rosebud, because the point is the impossibility of reducing a person to one answer.

films.title: Raging Bull
films.note: Gray's example of how specificity carries a film: the Mascagni score, the slow-motion open, the Chinese food at the table.

films.title: 2001: A Space Odyssey
films.note: Cited for its details (the sandwich dialogue, the lost cashmere sweater announcement) over its scale.

films.title: Amarcord
films.note: The one film Gray consciously had in mind before writing: Fellini's use of Mussolini's rise as backdrop to a town having a great time.

films.title: Unforgiven
films.note: Gray recalls the 1992 opening-day audience cheering the final massacre as proof a film's meaning isn't fixed by its maker.

films.title: Jaws
films.note: Gray pushes back on the 'end of New Hollywood' read: the mayor who'll let people get eaten to protect tourism revenue is a Trumpian figure hiding inside a blockbuster.

films.title: The Empire Strikes Back
films.note: Gray's example of a mythic core surviving inside pure escapism: he describes watching his kids react to the 'I am your father' reveal.

films.title: The Conversation
films.note: Gray recently rewatched it with his kids, who found its surveillance dread more frightening than he ever had.

films.title: Girl with a Suitcase
films.note: Gray's 'last great thing,' a hard-to-find Valerio Zurlini film with Claudia Cardinale that he's newly obsessed with.

Glass Onion: A Knives Out Mystery.title: Glass Onion: A Knives Out Mystery
